text: The surgical view shows the conduit appearing normal in size, with a notable presence of air adjacent to the JJ anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully removes the greater omentum to expose and visualize the JJ anastomosis site.
The surgeon visually identifies a perforation at the JJ anastomosis site.
The surgeon identifies the Roux limb and follows it back to the jejunojejunostomy (JJ) site.
The surgeon runs the BP limb, identifying the ligament of Treitz as a landmark in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines the common channel laparoscopically, confirming there is no evidence of obstruction. The ICG imaging appears clear and satisfactory.
Kira uses scissors to thin the tissue with a VESA sealer. A leak test is performed at the gastrojejunostomy (GJ) site, showing no abnormalities.
The surgeon creates an anastomosis in an anti-colic, anti-gastric fashion, visually aligning the intestinal segments in this orientation.
The surgeon places an omental patch over the repaired area, ensuring coverage and protection of the site.
The surgeon inspects the mucosa, which appears healthy and intact inside the surgical site.
The surgeon attempts to elevate the gastric remnant but encounters tension. To alleviate this, they release the attachment of the greater omentum to the stomach by carefully opening it.
A 20 French balloon G-tube is inserted and positioned, with the stomach being approximated to the posterior aspect of the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic washout of the abdominal cavity, placing multiple drains in the right upper quadrant, left upper quadrant, and pelvis.
